title: Side Hustles & The Value of Your Time
summary: Not all ways of making money are worth the same amount of your life.
doThisToday: If you have a side hustle, calculate your real hourly rate — profit divided by hours, including prep and cleanup time.

cards.prompt: Babysitting, mowing lawns, tutoring, reselling sneakers, making content — side hustles come in a lot of shapes.
cards.explanation: But not all side hustles are equally worth your time, and the sticker number ("I made $50!") doesn't tell the whole story on its own.

cards.prompt: You mow a lawn for $30 and it takes you 1 hour total. Your friend resells sneakers for $30 profit but it takes them 4 hours of searching, buying, and reselling. Whose time is being paid better?
options: Both are equal since the profit is the same
Your friend, because reselling is more skilled
cards.explanation: Same profit, very different value per hour. This is exactly why comparing side hustles by total profit alone can be misleading — the effective hourly rate tells the real story.

cards.prompt: A side hustle nets you $45 in profit and takes 3 hours total (including setup and cleanup). What's your effective hourly rate?
cards.explanation: $45 ÷ 3 hours = $15/hour. Always divide by TOTAL time, including the parts that don't feel like "work" — driving, prepping, packaging, posting.

cards.prompt: True or false: any side hustle is worth doing as long as it makes some money.
cards.explanation: A side hustle that pays $4/hour after all the time involved might not be worth it compared to a part-time job paying real minimum wage — or compared to the value of your free time and rest.

cards.prompt: You're offered a side hustle that pays well per hour, but it would eat into time you need for homework and sleep. What should factor into your decision?
options: Only the pay rate — more money is always the right call
The pay rate AND the real cost of losing sleep or falling behind in school, which have their own long-term value
Side hustles should always come before schoolwork
cards.explanation: Opportunity cost applies to time too, not just money. Trading sleep or grades for extra cash can cost you more in the long run than the dollars are worth.

cards.prompt: What usually makes a side hustle actually worth scaling up?
options: A decent hourly rate, low startup cost, and it doesn't wreck your schedule or sleep
It has to involve reselling something
It has to be something you saw on social media
cards.explanation: The best side hustles pay reasonably for your time, don't require much upfront money, and fit around the rest of your life — not just whatever's trending.

cards.prompt: Sort each side hustle into Good Time Investment or Bad Time Investment, based on effective hourly rate.

items.label: Reselling: $12 profit for 6 hours of hunting ($2/hr)
Contest entries: $8 profit for 5 hours of entering ($1.60/hr)

cards.explanation: The pattern: skilled or in-demand work (tutoring, lawn care, watching kids) tends to pay well per hour. Low-effort-sounding "passive" hustles (surveys, contest entries, hunting for resale deals) often pay far less per hour than they seem to promise.

cards.prompt: Your time isn't free just because you're a teenager. Treat it like the limited resource it is.
cards.explanation: Whether it's a job, a side hustle, or deciding what to do with a Saturday — the real question is always the same: is this worth my hours?
